title: Headaches, TBI, and Brain Fog in Daily Life
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Brain fog is often load plus injury, not laziness.

sections.heading: What Is Happening in Your System

content.text: Brain fog, frequent headaches, and cognitive slowdown in frontline work have multiple causes: stress overload, sleep debt, dehydration, screen time, and unrecognized concussion history. Your brain feels thick—like you're thinking through mud. Word-finding fails, focus slips, and headaches become a near-daily occurrence. For some, this includes undiagnosed mild TBI (traumatic brain injury) from falls, vehicle crashes, blasts, or repeated subconcussive hits that were never formally assessed.

items: Tension or pressure headaches (forehead, temples, back of head).
Light and sound sensitivity during headaches.
Brain fog: difficulty thinking clearly, finding words, or holding focus.
Mental slowdown: tasks take longer, decisions feel harder.
Difficulty tracking conversations or remembering instructions.
Worsening with screen time, bright lights, or noisy environments.

lines: Many frontline folks have unrecognized concussion history. Even 'minor' hits can cause lasting cognitive symptoms. If brain fog persists, get assessed.

sections.heading: Why It Gets Worse Under Frontline Load

content.text: Frontline work creates perfect conditions for headaches and brain fog: high cognitive demands under stress, chronic dehydration (forgetting to drink during shifts), poor sleep, screen overload (reports, computers, phones), and possible unrecognized concussion history. Your brain is running on low fuel, high demand, and inadequate recovery. Stress headaches come from sustained muscle tension (jaw, neck, scalp). Brain fog comes from a prefrontal cortex overwhelmed by too much input and not enough rest.

items: Screen overload: Reports, computers, phones all day—visual and cognitive fatigue.
Chronic dehydration: Forgetting to hydrate during long shifts.
Sleep disruption: Brain fog is one of the first symptoms of sleep debt.
Unrecognized concussion history: Falls, crashes, blasts, or repeated hits.
Sensory overload: Bright lights, sirens, radios—constant stimulation without breaks.

lines: Why this works: reducing total load and improving predictability lowers baseline arousal and restores decision quality.

content.text: You can't eliminate headaches and brain fog instantly, but you can reduce triggers and support recovery with structured breaks, hydration, and reduced cognitive load.

items: Structure tasks into 20-minute blocks: Work for 20 minutes, break for 2. Prevents cognitive overload.
Hydrate on a schedule: Set alarms. Drink 8oz every 2 hours during shifts. Dehydration causes headaches fast.
Reduce visual overload: Lower screen brightness, use blue-light filters, take eye breaks (20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 sec).
Log headache patterns: Track when headaches happen (time of day, triggers, hydration, sleep). Pattern = solution.
Address possible TBI: If you have concussion history and chronic symptoms, get neurocognitive assessment.
Use dark, quiet environments for breaks: Overstimulation worsens both headaches and fog.

lines: Field rule: make the action small enough that you can still do it on a bad day.

content.text: Household stability improves when everyone knows the plan before stress spikes.

items: Use short, single-step communication when fog is high: 'Can you take out the trash?' not 'Can you handle dinner prep and also take care of the trash and dog?'
Lower sensory demands at home: Dim lights, lower volumes, reduce competing inputs.
Support rest without guilt: Brain fog means the brain needs recovery, not more demands.
Encourage medical eval: Persistent headaches or worsening cognitive symptoms need assessment.

content.text: Self-management is a starting point, not a lifetime sentence. Escalate when function, safety, or relationships keep declining.

items: Headaches are increasing in frequency or intensity.
Brain fog persists despite sleep, hydration, and reduced load.
You have concussion history and chronic cognitive symptoms.
Headaches are accompanied by vision changes, balance issues, or nausea.
Work or home function is deteriorating due to cognitive slowdown.

content.text: Use the worksheet daily for one week. Measure trigger intensity, response size, and recovery time.

lines: Name one trigger, one body signal, and one reset action.
At end of week, keep what worked and remove what did not.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Headaches, TBI, and Brain Fog in Daily Life
worksheets.intro: Use this to track headaches, TBI effects, and cognitive fog in real time and build a repeatable response plan.

question_groups.label: Where did this pattern show up this week?
question_groups.hint: List specific moments at work, at home, or during transition.

question_groups.label: What did it cost you?
question_groups.hint: Include effect on body, mood, decisions, and relationships.

question_groups.label: What two actions will you run for the next 7 days?
question_groups.hint: Choose small, realistic actions you can perform under stress.

numbered_prompts.hint: What event, cue, or context activated this pattern?
What did your body do first?
Write the exact behavior you will use next time.

worksheets.closing_note: Track this daily for one week, then adjust based on what actually worked.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Headaches, TBI, and Brain Fog in Daily Life - Partner/Team Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this page for one short, practical check-in each week.

question_groups.label: What do others notice first when this pattern is active?
question_groups.hint: Ask for concrete observations, not labels.

question_groups.label: What support helps most?
question_groups.hint: List what helps and what makes it worse.

question_groups.label: What agreement will we practice this week?
question_groups.hint: One clear behavior from each side.

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset?
How long until re-engagement after a pause?
What specific repair happens if things go sideways?

worksheets.closing_note: Keep check-ins short, specific, and repeatable.
